This stage is a cross-cutting test harness layer around execution, transport, and sandbox infrastructure. It sits outside the main runtime path and proves that the system’s startup handshakes, long-lived RPC/process loops, and platform-specific teardown behavior all work correctly when exercised through real subprocesses, sockets, and protocol frames.

At the exec-server boundary, the shared test bootstrap and server harness launch real server instances, connect over WebSocket or stdio, and drive initialize, health, websocket, process, HTTP, and file-stream RPCs end to end. Handler and ExecProcess tests then validate session resumption, detached-process survival, stdin/output semantics, replay, and failure handling across local and remote backends. Filesystem suites cover shared local/remote behavior, Unix and Windows aliasing rules, sandbox enforcement, and URI/path edge cases.

Transport-focused tests verify listen URL parsing, Noise channel security, relay framing and ciphertext ordering, remote reconnect and authorization, and encrypted relay traffic. Remote-control transport tests cover chunking, enrollment, persistence, and recovery. Sandbox suites exercise policy generation and real execution bridges on Linux, macOS, Windows, and Wine, including stdio/UDS bridging, proxy-only networking, and helper argv shaping. Together these harnesses ensure the system’s execution substrate behaves consistently across transports, hosts, and isolation mechanisms.
